constructors-and-object-state.label: Constructors and Object State
constructors-and-object-state.summary: Add `__init__`, store attributes on `self`, and see how different objects keep different state.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.title: Add a Car constructor
try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.prompt: In `models/car.py`, update the `Car` class so it has `__init__(self, make, model, miles)`. Store the values on `self.make`, `self.model`, and `self.miles`. In `main.py`, create `Car("Nissan", "Leaf", 6000)`, then print the car's make and miles on separate lines.
try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.hint: The constructor receives starting values and stores them on `self`.

help.concept: `__init__` runs when a new object is created. It stores starting values as attributes on that object.
help.hint_2: After creating the car, print `car.make` and `car.miles`.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.starterCode:
from models.car import Car

# After updating the constructor, create a car and print two attributes.

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

# After updating the constructor, create a car and print two attributes.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.solutionCode:
from models.car import Car

car = Car("Nissan", "Leaf", 6000)

print(car.make)
print(car.miles)

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

car = Car("Nissan", "Leaf", 6000)

print(car.make)
print(car.miles)

1.message: Car should be constructible with make, model, and miles.

2.message: Store make, model, and miles as attributes on the object.

3.message: Print the requested attributes on separate lines.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.title: Create two cars with different attributes
try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.prompt: Keep the `Car` constructor. In `main.py`, create `Car("Subaru", "Outback", 40000)` and `Car("Mini", "Cooper", 15000)`. Print the first car's model on one line and the second car's make on the next line.
try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.hint: Create two different Car objects, then use dot notation to read different attributes.

help.concept: Each object stores its own attributes. Two Car objects can have different make, model, and miles values.
help.hint_1: Use two variables, such as `outback` and `mini`.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.starterCode:
from models.car import Car

# Create two Car objects with different values.
# Print one attribute from each.

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

# Create two Car objects with different values.
# Print one attribute from each.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.solutionCode:
from models.car import Car

outback = Car("Subaru", "Outback", 40000)
mini = Car("Mini", "Cooper", 15000)

print(outback.model)
print(mini.make)

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

outback = Car("Subaru", "Outback", 40000)
mini = Car("Mini", "Cooper", 15000)

print(outback.model)
print(mini.make)

1.message: Car should accept make, model, and miles.

2.message: Each Car object should keep its own attributes.

3.message: Create at least two Car objects.

4.message: Print the requested attributes on separate lines.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.title: Show that each car keeps its own state
try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.prompt: Create `Car("Hyundai", "Elantra", 25000)` and `Car("Chevrolet", "Bolt", 9000)`. Change only the first car's `miles` attribute to `25100`, then print both cars' miles on separate lines. This shows that changing one object does not change the other.
try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.hint: Assign to the first object's attribute only: `first_car.miles = 25100`.

help.concept: Object state belongs to one object. Updating one car's miles should not update every car.
help.hint_2: Update the first car only, then print both `miles` values.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.starterCode:
from models.car import Car

# Create two cars.
# Change only the first car's miles.
# Print both miles values.

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

# Create two cars.
# Change only the first car's miles.
# Print both miles values.

try_constructors_and_object_state_sketch.solutionCode:
from models.car import Car

first_car = Car("Hyundai", "Elantra", 25000)
second_car = Car("Chevrolet", "Bolt", 9000)

first_car.miles = 25100

print(first_car.miles)
print(second_car.miles)

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

first_car = Car("Hyundai", "Elantra", 25000)
second_car = Car("Chevrolet", "Bolt", 9000)

first_car.miles = 25100

print(first_car.miles)
print(second_car.miles)

1.message: Car should accept make, model, and miles.

2.message: Each Car object should keep its own attributes.

3.message: Create at least two Car objects.

4.message: Print both miles values on separate lines.

mc-constructors-and-object-state-storing.title: What does __init__ store?
mc-constructors-and-object-state-storing.prompt: Which statements correctly describe the constructor and object state? Choose all that apply.
mc-constructors-and-object-state-storing.hint: Think about values passed into the class call and stored on `self`.

help.concept: `__init__` receives starting values and stores them as attributes on the new object.
help.hint_1: Look for choices about `self` and per-object values.
help.hint_2: Reject choices that say every object shares one copy of every value.

options.a: `__init__` runs when a new object is created.
options.b: `self.make = make` stores a value on that object.
options.c: Every object must share one global set of attributes.
options.d: `__init__` can only print values, not store them.

sc-constructors-and-object-state-purpose.title: Why use self?
sc-constructors-and-object-state-purpose.hint: `self` refers to the object being created or updated.

help.concept: `self` lets code inside the class store values on this specific object.
help.hint_1: Think about where `make`, `model`, and `miles` should live.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer about this object.

options.a: `self` refers to the specific object receiving the attribute.
options.b: `self` means the value is stored in every file.

fb-constructors-and-object-state-init-na.title: Name the constructor
fb-constructors-and-object-state-init-na.prompt: Fill in the method name Python calls when a new object is created.
fb-constructors-and-object-state-init-na.hint: It starts and ends with two underscores.

help.concept: `__init__` is the constructor method Python calls during object creation.
help.hint_1: The name has two underscores on each side.

dr-constructors-and-object-state-flow.title: Order the constructor flow
dr-constructors-and-object-state-flow.hint: Start with the class definition, then create an object.

help.concept: The constructor flow is: define `__init__`, call the class with values, store values on `self`, then read attributes.
help.hint_1: The class must know how to receive values before you pass values in.
help.hint_2: Reading attributes happens after the object exists.

tokens.t2: Create an object with starting values

sketch-0.title: A constructor receives starting values
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
A class shell can create an object, but it cannot store useful values yet.

To give each object starting values, add a constructor method named `__init__`:

```python
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.pages = pages
```

When you create a book object, Python calls `__init__` automatically:

```python
book = Book("The Hobbit", "J. R. R. Tolkien", 310)
```

The values inside `Book(...)` flow into the constructor parameters.

sketch-1.title: Attributes store values on this object
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
Inside the constructor, assignments like these create attributes:

```python
self.title = title
self.author = author
self.pages = pages
```

`self` means "this object."

So `self.title = title` means: store the title value on this book object.

Later, `main.py` can read those attributes with dot notation:

```python
book = Book("The Hobbit", "J. R. R. Tolkien", 310)
print(book.title)
print(book.pages)
```

Attributes are how an object remembers its state.

sketch-2.title: Each object owns its own state
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
A constructor can create many objects from the same class, and each object keeps its own attribute values.

```python
first_book = Book("The Hobbit", "J. R. R. Tolkien", 310)
second_book = Book("Charlotte's Web", "E. B. White", 192)

print(first_book.title)
print(second_book.title)
```

Both objects come from `Book`, but their state is different.

That is why constructors matter: they let one class create many useful objects without hardcoding the same values into every object.
